name: Xenu Story
description: The Xenu Story, also known as Incident II, is a central tenet of Scientology's advanced teachings. It describes a galactic ruler named Xenu who, 75 million years ago, brought billions of beings to Earth (then called Teegeeack) in spacecraft resembling Douglas DC-8s. These beings were paralyzed, stacked around volcanoes, and detonated with hydrogen bombs. Their disembodied spirits, called thetans, clustered together and attached themselves to humans, causing spiritual harm and negatively impacting their lives. The story is revealed to Scientologists who have reached Operating Thetan Level III (OT III) and is considered a confidential and sacred teaching. Scientology founder L. Ron Hubbard is credited with originating the Xenu Story. Proponents see it as a literal historical account explaining the origins of human suffering and the need for Scientology auditing to clear these attached thetans. Mainstream scholars and critics dismiss the Xenu Story as science fiction, a fabrication, or a metaphor. They point to the lack of any independent corroborating evidence from geological, archaeological, or historical records. The Xenu Story is often cited as an example of the bizarre and unconventional beliefs held by Scientologists and is used to criticize the organization's practices and high financial demands associated with advanced levels.
subtitle: Galactic overlord and exploding thetans.

claims.claim: Xenu brought billions of beings to Earth 75 million years ago.
claims.evidence: Scientology's OT III materials detail the account.
claims.criticism: No scientific evidence supports this claim; geological and archaeological records contradict it.

claims.claim: These beings were killed by hydrogen bombs detonated in volcanoes.
claims.evidence: Scientology's OT III materials explicitly describe this event.
claims.criticism: No volcanic activity or geological evidence supports the simultaneous detonation of hydrogen bombs across Earth 75 million years ago.

claims.claim: The disembodied spirits (thetans) attach themselves to humans, causing spiritual harm.
claims.evidence: Auditing processes in Scientology are designed to address and clear these thetans.
claims.criticism: There is no scientific basis for the existence of thetans or their ability to attach to humans and cause harm. This concept lacks empirical support.

keyFigures.name: L. Ron Hubbard
keyFigures.role: Scientology founder and author of the Xenu Story

keyFigures.name: Lawrence Wright
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Going Clear,' a critical examination of Scientology

keyFigures.name: Jon Atack
keyFigures.role: Former Scientologist and critic of the organization

timeline.event: L. Ron Hubbard writes and disseminates the OT III materials containing the Xenu Story.
The Xenu Story becomes publicly known through leaked documents and court cases.
The Xenu Story continues to be a controversial and debated aspect of Scientology beliefs.
